content.text:
Modelling and Optimization
Semester:
3  |
Type:
UEF32  |
VHS:
63h  |
Weekly hours:
4h30  |
Lecture:
1h30  |
Tutorial:
1h30  |
Lab:
1h30
Personal work VHS:
21h  |
Coefficient:
3  |
Credits:
4
Teaching Objectives
This module introduces the fundamental concepts of constraint problem modelling and optimisation/decision problem solving, with particular emphasis on combinatorial optimisation. It covers:
Modelling Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP) and solving them with classical algorithms (backtracking, forward checking, heuristics) and dedicated solvers. Special attention is paid to the link between CSPs and integer linear programming (ILP).
Resolution methods for large search spaces: local search techniques such as simulated annealing, genetic algorithms, dynamic programming, and branch and bound (applied to knapsack, inventory management, and scheduling problems).
Course Content (21h)
Chapter I – Modelling Constraint Satisfaction Problems (CSP)
Definition of a constraint satisfaction problem
Formal model: variables, domains, constraints
CSP examples: sudoku, timetabling, assignment
Chapter II – CSP Typology
Classical CSPs (binary, non-binary)
Discrete vs continuous CSPs
Hard vs soft constraints
Dynamic and incremental CSPs
Chapter III – CSP Resolution Algorithms
Backtracking methods
Forward checking
Heuristics: most constrained variable, least constraining value
Performance comparison
Chapter IV – Consistency and Complexity
Arc-consistency concept
Domain reduction of variables
Path consistency and k-consistency
Complexity analysis of filtering algorithms
Adaptation to structured problems (trees, grids, networks)
Chapter V – Integer Linear Programming and CSP
Review of integer linear programming
Modelling a CSP as a linear problem
Examples: assignment, colouring, logical problems
Advantages and limitations of this approach
Chapter VI – Local Search for CSPs
Motivation and context (large search spaces)
Algorithms: min-conflicts, simulated annealing, tabu search, and genetic algorithms
Neighbourhood concept, state evaluation
Applications to planning and placement
Chapter VII – Dynamic Programming
Principle of dynamic programming: sub-problems and reuse
Recursive modelling and tabulation
Applications:
Knapsack problem (0-1 and fractional)
Inventory management (optimal replenishment)
Task scheduling (with or without precedence constraints)
Assessment
Final examination (in person): 60%
Continuous assessment (CC): 40% — written tests, personal work
